channel.title: Science on Alien Truth Social
channel.description: Recent content in Science on Alien Truth Social

item.title: UFO religion
item.description:
A UFO religion, also known as a UFO cult, is a religion where belief in extraterrestrial entities and communication with them is central. These religions often posit that aliens are interested in humanity’s welfare and can help solve Earth’s problems with their advanced technology and spirituality. Their origins are linked to science fiction, ufology, and UFO sighting narratives. Some historians consider the Aetherius Society or the “I AM” Activity to be the first UFO religions.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/UFO_religion

item.title: Nibiru cataclysm
item.description:
The Nibiru cataclysm is a predicted disastrous encounter between Earth and a large planetary object, often referred to as Nibiru or Planet X. This doomsday scenario was first proposed in 1995 by Nancy Lieder, who claimed to receive messages from extraterrestrials. The idea has since spread through various internet groups and has been linked to the 2012 phenomenon and other astronomical events. Astronomers and planetary scientists have widely rejected the Nibiru cataclysm as pseudoscience, stating there is no scientific evidence to support such claims.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nibiru_cataclysm

item.title: Earthquake light
item.description:
Earthquake lights are luminous optical phenomena reported in association with tectonic stress, seismic activity, or volcanic eruptions. While historical accounts date back to the 869 Jōgan earthquake, the phenomenon remains a subject of ongoing scientific debate regarding its validity and underlying causes. Proposed theories range from piezoelectric generation and rock stress-induced ionization to non-seismic explanations, such as power grid failures or atmospheric conditions during storms. Skeptics, however, continue to challenge the existence of these lights, noting that many reports lack direct evidence and were once conflated with paranormal or UFO-related claims.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Earthquake_light

item.title: Unidentified flying object
item.description:
An unidentified flying object (UFO), also referred to as an unidentified anomalous phenomenon (UAP), is any aerial object or phenomenon that cannot be immediately identified or explained. While historical reports of unusual aerial sightings date back to ancient times, the term gained cultural prominence following World War II, leading to various government investigations, such as the U.S. Air Force’s Project Blue Book. Although scientific and skeptical organizations, including the Committee for Skeptical Inquiry, consistently find that the majority of these reports are misidentifications of natural phenomena, human technology, or hoaxes, a small percentage remain unexplained. In recent years, entities such as NASA’s UAP independent study team and the Department of Defense’s All-domain Anomaly Resolution Office have continued to analyze data regarding these encounters.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unidentified_flying_object

item.title: Interdimensional UFO hypothesis
item.description:
The interdimensional UFO hypothesis (IUH) proposes that UFO sightings are not necessarily extraterrestrial in origin but result from encounters with entities or phenomena from coexisting, unseen dimensions. Notable ufologists such as Meade Layne, John Keel, J. Allen Hynek, and Jacques Vallée have advanced this theory, suggesting that these anomalies have appeared throughout history under various guises, including mythological or supernatural events. Proponents argue that the recurring patterns and characteristics of these sightings, which often defy conventional space-travel physics, are better explained by the existence of “interlocking universes” rather than planetary visitation.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Interdimensional_UFO_hypothesis

item.title: Cryptoterrestrial hypothesis
item.description:
The cryptoterrestrial hypothesis suggests that unidentified aerial phenomena (UFOs) are evidence of an advanced, indigenous civilization hidden on Earth rather than extraterrestrial visitors. The concept draws from late 19th-century literature and mid-20th-century fringe theories, such as the “Shaver Mystery,” to propose that these beings operate from subterranean or hidden environments. While scholars often frame the hypothesis as a thought experiment, the idea remains a recurring theme in modern ufology, appearing in recent discussions by figures like Luis Elizondo. Additionally, the theme of hidden Earth-based civilizations is frequently explored in popular media, including films like The Abyss and Nope, which utilize these narratives to create speculative science-fiction scenarios.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cryptoterrestrial_hypothesis

item.title: Aligned, Multiple-transient Events in the First Palomar Sky Survey
item.description:
In October 2025, astronomer Beatriz Villarroel and psychologist Stephen Bruehl published two companion papers in Publications of the Astronomical Society of the Pacific and Scientific Reports suggesting that transient light events in 1950s Palomar Observatory photographic plates could be linked to nuclear testing or unidentified anomalous phenomena. The research faced widespread skepticism from the scientific community, with experts such as Michael Wiescher and Nigel Hambly suggesting that the anomalies were likely due to plate defects, meteors, or other prosaic environmental factors. A formal rejoinder by physicists Kevin Knuth, Wesley Andres Watters, and Laura Dominé further challenged the authors’ methodology, citing issues with data preparation and a lack of microscopic analysis. While some independent analysis by researchers like Ivo Busko has noted similar transients in other archival datasets, the original papers were notably rejected by the preprint server arXiv for lacking sufficient scholarly rigor.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aligned%2C_Multiple-transient_Events_in_the_First_Palomar_Sky_Survey

item.title: Moths at Attracted by Floodlight and the 'Rod' Phenomenon
item.description:
In cryptozoology and ufology, “rods” are elongated visual artifacts seen in photographs and videos. While some proponents claim they are paranormal or alien in nature, optical analyses suggest they are insects captured over multiple wing beats, creating an illusion of a single, rod-like body. Investigations, including a documentary by China Central Television, have demonstrated that these phenomena are often caused by motion blur from slow camera exposure times and the way flying insects are recorded. José Escamilla was a notable proponent who claimed to have filmed rods, interpreting them as alien life forms.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rod_%28optical_phenomenon%29

item.title: Morris Ketchum Jessup
item.description:
Morris Ketchum Jessup was an American ufologist with a Master of Science degree in astronomy. He is best known for his writings on UFOs, including his 1955 book The Case for the UFO. Jessup’s work explored theories linking ancient monuments to prehistoric superscience and speculated about the nature of UFOs. His death in 1959 was ruled a suicide, but circumstances surrounding it have fueled conspiracy theories, particularly concerning the Philadelphia Experiment.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Morris_K._Jessup

item.title: The Extraterrestrial UFO Hypothesis (ETH)
item.description:
The extraterrestrial hypothesis (ETH) proposes that some unidentified flying objects (UFOs) are physical spacecraft piloted by intelligent extraterrestrial beings. While historical speculation about alien visitors dates back centuries, the modern ETH gained traction in the late 1940s and 1950s, influenced by early science fiction and widespread UFO sightings. Despite some historical government and private studies suggesting extraterrestrial origins, the scientific community largely attributes UFO reports to misinterpretations, hoaxes, or common phenomena, with no definitive physical evidence of alien visitation found.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Extraterrestrial_UFO_hypothesis

item.title: Megatron: The UFO-Shaped Restaurant
item.description:
The Megatron was a flying saucer-designed building in Cambridgeshire, UK, constructed in 1990 as a restaurant. It was conceived by local businessman Danny Blundell and designed by Graham Campbell, featuring innovations like touchscreen ordering. Upon opening, locals mistook it for a UFO, prompting police investigation. The restaurant closed in 1992 due to high costs and was later taken over by McDonald’s, before ultimately being demolished in 2008.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Megatron_%28building%29

item.title: Xenoarchaeology
item.description:
Xenoarchaeology is a hypothetical field of study concerned with the material remains of extraterrestrial cultures, primarily existing in science fiction. It is a branch of xenology that aims to reconstruct and interpret past alien civilizations. The field is not currently practiced by mainstream archaeologists due to a lack of actual material to study. Xenoarchaeology is also referred to as astroarchaeology or exoarchaeology and encompasses related concepts like SETI and SETA.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Xenoarchaeology

item.title: Integratron
item.description:
The Integratron is a cupola structure in Landers, California, designed by ufologist George Van Tassel, who claimed it could rejuvenate, provide anti-gravity, and enable time travel. Van Tassel stated he received instructions for its construction from Venusians and began building it in 1957, with the structure erected in 1959. After Van Tassel’s death, the building was acquired by sisters who now offer “sound baths” within the structure, promoting it as an acoustically perfect sound chamber. The Integratron was added to the National Register of Historic Places in April 2018.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Integratron

item.title: British Rail flying saucer patent
item.description:
The British Rail flying saucer, officially termed “space vehicle,” was a proposed interplanetary spacecraft designed by Charles Osmond Frederick. A patent application was filed for this craft by British Rail in December 1970 and granted in March 1973. The design, which evolved from a lifting platform to a large passenger craft, was to be powered by controlled thermonuclear fusion using laser beams. This concept was later examined by nuclear scientists who deemed it unworkable and inefficient, noting that many of the required technologies had not yet been discovered.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/British_Rail_flying_saucer

item.title: America's Nuclear Flying Saucer
item.description:
The Lenticular Reentry Vehicle (LRV) was an experimental nuclear warhead delivery system developed during the Cold War by North American Aviation. Research into lenticular-shaped vehicles for re-entry characteristics began in the late 1950s, with the LRV project being classified in 1962 and declassified in 1999. The LRV was described as a 40-foot half-saucer capable of supporting a crew of four for six-week orbital missions, powered by a rocket engine and an onboard nuclear reactor. While its existence may lend credence to military flying saucer theories, its described flight characteristics are more akin to a standard space capsule.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lenticular_Reentry_Vehicle
